Dreidel: [{'Dreidel': 'A dreidel is a traditional spinning top used in the Jewish holiday of Hanukkah, typically made of wood or plastic, with a wooden handle and a flat, rounded top. It has a distinctive shape and size, and it features a single Hebrew letter and its corresponding Hebrew script on one of its sides. The letter "Hay" or "Shin" is prominently displayed on the top face of the dreidel, with the Hebrew script "ה" (hay) or "ש" (shin) clearly visible.'}]
Gimel: A Hebrew letter resembling a figure with two branches. It looks similar to the greek letter lambda.
Hay: [{'Hay': 'A stylized Hebrew letter "Hay" with a curved top and a straight bottom, in a blue color on a textured background, resembling a Hebrew letter "Hay".'}]
Nun: [{'Nun': 'A dreidel, a four-sided spinning top used in the Jewish holiday of Hanukkah, with the Hebrew letter "nun" painted or printed on the flat face of the dreidel, characterized by a single vertical line with a horizontal line at the top, and easily distinguishable from other Hebrew letters due to its unique shape.'}]
Shin: [{'Shin': 'A stylized representation of the Hebrew letter "Shin" with a square base and a curved top, typically featuring a pointed tip at the top, and not resembling a dreidel with the Hebrew letter "Gimel" written on it.'}]
Spinning Dreidel: A dreidel in motion, appearing blurred due to its spin.
